content:
A profession gives your value a name and somewhere to go. That is not the same as creating it

4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

AI-assisted illustration created for this essayA sales director I know once told me that the most satisfying moment of his twenty-year career had nothing to do with hitting a target.
A former client called him years after they had worked together and said, “That judgment you made back then saved us.”
No one was still tracking the decision. It belonged to another reporting period, another set of spreadsheets. Yet it had continued to matter in the client’s life.
That was what stayed with him. His judgment had shaped a real decision and changed what happened next. He had also been the one responsible if it went wrong.
The call told him more about the value of his work than any performance review had.
We are used to reading value through professional identity. Doctors have licences, lawyers have credentials, managers have authority, and programmers have technical expertise. These things matter. They determine who is allowed into a profession, what someone is trusted to do, and often how much they can charge.
The trouble begins when the qualification, title, or salary becomes indistinguishable from the value behind it.
A medical licence admits someone to the profession. It does not give a new doctor the judgment of a clinician who has spent years handling difficult cases. That judgment develops through patients who do not fit the textbook, decisions made with incomplete information, errors remembered long after everyone else has moved on, and nights when there was no senior colleague available to take over.
The profession makes that judgment usable. It gives it structure, resources, and legitimacy. But the value takes shape in the work itself.
The same pattern exists outside paid employment. The word “father” names a relationship; it does not tell us what a man’s presence has meant in his child’s life. A retired person explaining history online may have no institutional standing, but a stranger may finally understand something because of the way he explained it.
Value takes shape when someone’s attention, judgment, or care has a real effect on another person’s life or work.
This is becoming harder to see as AI changes professional work.
Some tasks that once required years of training can now be completed with software assistance. Skills that were scarce are becoming easier to access. Work that supported a certain salary may be produced faster and sold for less.
The financial effects are real. A person may lose income, status, bargaining power, or an entire career path. Telling someone in that position that their “true value” remains intact can sound evasive, especially when the bills still have to be paid.
But beneath the economic fear is often another question: Did all those years still count?
If the market no longer pays as much for my skill, was the effort wasted? If a machine can produce a similar output in minutes, what happened to the person who spent twenty years learning how to do it?
Those questions make sense if value has been stored entirely in the profession. Once the profession changes, everything seems to go with it.
But those twenty years did not happen inside a title.
They happened in awkward conversations, bad calls that had to be repaired, situations where the available information was poor, and moments when someone still had to decide. They happened in the consequences that followed.
AI can alter the price of a skill. An organisation can remove a role. Neither can undo the situations a person has already lived through or the judgment formed by living through them.
What can disappear is the old route between that judgment and the world.
That loss should not be minimised. A new route may be difficult to find, and it may not pay as well. Still, losing the route is different from losing everything that once travelled through it.
The sales director told me he has started asking himself a different question. He used to wonder what his profession was worth now. These days, he asks where his judgment actually entered the market and whether it changed anything for anyone.
He said the second question feels heavier, but also steadier.
The market will continue to reprice professions. Organisations will change, and tools will take over work that once belonged to people. Experience may not retain its old economic value. It may need another role, another organisation, or another kind of work before it becomes useful again.
But the years themselves have not vanished.
A badge can expire. It cannot take those years with it.
